title: Aztlan and the Eagle on the Cactus
bullets: The Mexica spoke Nahuatl, a language of the Uto-Aztecan family that also includes Hopi, Comanche, and Shoshone, making them linguistic outsiders when they arrived in a basin dominated by Oto-Manguean and Mayan speakers.
Around 1100 CE the Mexica left the legendary homeland Aztlan, led south over two centuries by the war god Huitzilopochtli carried in a portable shrine on a priest's back.
The Mexica arrived in the Basin of Mexico as despised latecomers in a basin already crowded with dozens of competing city-states, each called an altepetl.

Driven from Chapultepec by a coalition of basin city-states around 1248, the Mexica were settled by Culhuacan on Tizapan, a snake-infested wasteland meant to kill them; they ate the snakes and thrived.
The Mexica vassalage at Culhuacan ended in catastrophe when priests sacrificed and flayed a Culhua princess offered as a diplomatic bride, forcing another flight.
In 1325 Mexica priests saw an eagle on a prickly pear cactus devouring a snake on a marshy island in Lake Texcoco, the sign Huitzilopochtli had promised; the image survives today on the Mexican flag.
The founding site of Tenochtitlan was, by every objective measure, terrible: a mosquito-ridden salt marsh with no farmland, no fresh water, no stone, and no timber.

The Mexica negotiated permission to settle from the Tepanec king Tezozomoc, becoming his formal vassals in exchange for tribute and military service, with a second community, Tlatelolco, founded on a neighboring island shortly after.

title: From Vassal to Hegemon
bullets: For 103 years the Mexica paid tribute to Azcapotzalco and fought as Tepanec mercenaries, growing wealthy enough that individual nobles had their own tributary grants while Tenochtitlan's population reached perhaps 30,000.

In 1376 the elders chose Acamapichtli as the first tlatoani, specifically because his half-Culhua descent gave the Mexica a claim to Toltec blood, the gold standard of royal legitimacy across Nahuatl-speaking central Mexico.

Tezozomoc's death in 1426 triggered a succession crisis: his son Maxtla seized the throne, killed rivals, and assassinated the Mexica tlatoani Chimalpopoca in 1427, leaving Tenochtitlan surrounded and leaderless.
The Mexica elders chose Itzcoatl, son of Acamapichtli by a slave woman, not because he was a legitimate heir but because he was the only candidate willing to fight rather than surrender.

Itzcoatl forged the Triple Alliance in 1428 with the fugitive Texcocan king Nezahualcoyotl and the smaller city of Tlacopan, splitting future tribute in a 2:2:1 ratio that held for nearly ninety years.

After a 114-day siege, Azcapotzalco fell; Maxtla was sacrificed at the founding ceremony of the Triple Alliance, his heart burned as an offering to Huitzilopochtli in front of the assembled allied armies.

Itzcoatl ordered the burning of the codices around 1430, destroying older painted histories that showed the Mexica as poor migrants, and commissioned new ones that backdated Mexica greatness and recast Huitzilopochtli as the sun itself.
The man behind the rewriting was Tlacaelel, Itzcoatl's nephew, who held the invented office of cihuacoatl for fifty-nine years across four reigns and shaped what the Aztec Empire ultimately became.

title: Tlacaelel and the Engine of Conquest
bullets: Tlacaelel was offered the throne three times and refused each time, judging that being kingmaker across four reigns was more durable than being a king who could be killed or poisoned.

Tlacaelel's central religious reform promoted Huitzilopochtli from a narrow tribal war god to the sun itself, making the sun's daily rising dependent on a steady supply of warrior blood and turning every act of imperial expansion into a religious obligation.
The debt-and-payment cosmology framed human sacrifice as repaying what the gods had voluntarily contracted at Teotihuacan when they sacrificed themselves to set the fifth sun in motion.

Tlacaelel institutionalized the Flower Wars around 1455 as a permanent mechanism: scheduled ritualized warfare against Tlaxcala, Cholula, and Huejotzingo to capture high-quality warriors for sacrifice when full conquest campaigns slowed.
The Flower Wars created eighty years of accumulated Tlaxcalan grievance that would, in 1519, hand Cortés his decisive ally and effectively doom the empire the Flower Wars were designed to sustain.

Motecuzoma I pushed the empire well beyond the Basin of Mexico for the first time, conquering the Huastec Gulf Coast and reaching Oaxacan Mixtec and Zapotec territories, with tribute lists adding rubber, cotton, vanilla, gold, jade, and cochineal.

After a catastrophic 1449 flood, Nezahualcoyotl designed the Albarradon de Nezahualcoyotl, a 10-mile stone dike across Lake Texcoco, the largest hydraulic engineering project in the pre-Columbian Americas, separating the brackish lake from the freshwater chinampa zone.

The One Rabbit famine of 1454 collapsed harvests for four consecutive years; people sold themselves into slavery and gave their children to coastal Totonacs for maize, permanently expanding the institution of slavery in Aztec society.

By 1469, the Aztec Empire stretched from the Gulf Coast to parts of the Pacific, drawing tribute from over 200 conquered towns, with Tlacaelel still in his office outlasting his brother Motecuzoma I by nearly twenty years.

title: The Floating City
bullets: By the 1490s Tenochtitlan held between 150,000 and 250,000 residents, larger than London, Paris, or Seville, and the metropolitan complex with Tlatelolco may have reached 300,000.

The natural high ground of the island in 1325 totaled perhaps half a square mile; by 1500 the urbanized area was over five square miles, the difference made by chinampas, dredged earth, and pile-driven fill built by hand over two centuries.

A well-managed chinampa could produce up to seven harvests per year, feeding perhaps fifteen people per acre, and the chinampa zone south of Tenochtitlan covered roughly 35 square miles at its peak, roughly twice the size of developed Manhattan.

Three causeways connected the island to the mainland, each punctuated by removable wooden bridges over channels left for defensive use; the bridges could be lifted out to isolate the city, a feature the Spanish would learn about the hard way during La Noche Triste.

The Chapultepec Aqueduct, designed by Nezahualcoyotl and completed around 1466, was a double-pipe stuccoed stone system running 2.5 miles from freshwater springs, built with parallel pipes so one could be cleaned while the other carried water.

Ahuitzotl's Acuecuexatl aqueduct of 1499 overflowed in 1500, raised the lake level several feet, flooded large sections of the city, and may have caused the injuries from which Ahuitzotl never fully recovered.

The Sacred Precinct enclosed 78 separate buildings including the Templo Mayor, a tzompantli skull rack confirmed archaeologically in the 2010s, a ceremonial ball court, and the calmecac school for noble boys.

The Coyolxauhqui Stone, an eleven-foot circular relief set flush with the plaza floor at the base of the Huitzilopochtli stairway, was designed so that every sacrificial body tumbling down the steps landed on it, reenacting the cosmic dismemberment of the moon goddess.

The Tlatelolco Market sold perhaps 40,000 to 80,000 daily visitors worth of goods organized by commodity, with on-site magistrates adjudicating disputes, and ran on cacao beans as small-denomination currency and cotton quachtli cloth as the larger denomination.

title: The Sun That Demanded Blood
bullets: The Aztecs believed the current world was its fifth age, created at Teotihuacan when the scabrous god Nanahuatzin threw himself into the fire without hesitation and was reborn as the sun, while the wealthier Tecuciztecatl hesitated and became only the moon.
Because the gods then sacrificed themselves to set the new sun moving, the Aztecs understood human sacrifice as repaying a cosmic debt, keeping the fifth sun in motion, not as gratuitous killing.

The Aztecs made pilgrimages to the ruins of Teotihuacan, named them the place where the gods were born, collected artifacts from the site for ritual reuse, and incorporated the pyramids into their creation mythology as the birthplace of the current world.

Tlaloc, the rain god paired with Huitzilopochtli at the Templo Mayor's twin shrines, demanded child sacrifices during the dry season, their crying intended to bring rain; archaeologists have confirmed the buried remains of sacrificed children in deposits beneath his shrine.
Quetzalcoatl's legend included a vow to return in the cyclical year called One Reed; the Year One Reed of the early 16th century corresponded to 1519, the year Cortés arrived on the Gulf Coast.

Xipe Totec's annual festival Tlacaxipehualiztli required priests to wear the flayed skins of sacrificed victims for twenty days as the skin rotted away, enacting the agricultural metaphor of the new corn stalk emerging from the dry husk.

The tonalpohualli (260-day ritual calendar) and the xiuhpohualli (365-day solar calendar) interlocked into a 52-year cycle called xiuhmolpilli, at the end of which the world might fail to begin a new cycle.

The New Fire Ceremony of 1507 required all fires across the empire to be extinguished, pregnant women locked in granaries, children kept awake all night, and new fire drilled on a sacrificial victim's sternum atop the Hill of the Star before relay runners relit the continent from a single flame.

title: Ladders and Cages
bullets: The tlatoani was not strictly hereditary but elected by a senior noble council from the pool of royal descendants; the system produced a reliable string of capable warrior-kings, with Tizoc, probably poisoned after five weak years, as the main exception.

A commoner who captured four enemy warriors in battle qualified for the title of eagle warrior or jaguar warrior, and one who excelled enough could be elevated to cuauhpilli, a meritocratic nobility distinct from but nearly equal to the hereditary pipiltin.

Aztec slavery was not a hereditary caste: children of tlacotin were born free, slavery resulted from criminal punishment, debt, or self-sale during famine, and slaves could own property, marry, and purchase their freedom.

Calmecac students, sons of the pipiltin and selected commoner boys, lived at temple schools and mastered religious ritual, calendar mathematics, astronomy, codex painting, Nahuatl rhetoric, history, and law through fasting, sleep deprivation, and physical self-mortification.
Macuilxochitzin, daughter of the cihuacoatl Tlacaelel, composed Nahuatl poetry celebrated in her own lifetime; her surviving poems are among the most beautiful texts in the Aztec corpus.

Nezahualcoyotl of Texcoco ruled for forty-one years, designed the Albarradon, wrote philosophical poetry that rejected the dominant sacrificial cosmology, and built the most legally sophisticated court in central Mexico, whose 80-article legal code was partially adopted across the empire.

The pochteca, the hereditary long-distance merchant guild, sent armed trading expeditions from their own calpulli neighborhoods to the Soconusco cacao coast, the Maya Yucatan, and reportedly as far as the American Southwest, returning with luxury goods and political intelligence.

Aztec medicine, practiced by specialists called ticitl, maintained working knowledge of over a thousand medicinal plants; the Badianus Manuscript of 1552 preserved a fraction of this tradition and is the oldest medical text from the Americas.

title: Ahuitzotl and the High Tide
bullets: Axayacatl conquered Tlatelolco in 1473 by killing its tlatoani Moquihuix during the assault on the sister city's Templo Mayor, annexing the most important marketplace in central Mexico under Tenochtitlan's direct administration.

In 1479 the Aztec army of roughly 32,000 met the Tarascan Purépecha at Tlaximaloyan and lost over 20,000 men, the worst military defeat in Mexica history; the Tarascan frontier was never crossed again.

Axayacatl commissioned the Sun Stone in 1479, a basalt disc nearly 12 feet across and weighing 24 tons, now the most famous single object of pre-Columbian art, intended as a sacrificial platform in the Sacred Precinct.

Tizoc reigned for only five years before being almost certainly assassinated by poison in 1486, probably orchestrated by the then-octogenarian Tlacaelel who had outlasted four tlatoani and recognized a king too weak to feed the sacrificial system.

Ahuitzotl rededicated the Phase VII Templo Mayor in 1487 with thousands of sacrificial victims, conquered the Soconusco Pacific coast by 1494 to secure the empire's luxury cacao supply, and pushed the empire to its maximum extent of roughly 80,000 square miles across 38 tributary provinces.

The annual tribute recorded in the post-conquest Codex Mendoza included over 7,000 tons of maize and beans, 200,000 cotton mantles, 32,000 reams of paper, 16,000 rubber balls, and an extraordinary range of regional specialties.

The Inca Empire, building its own continental-scale state simultaneously, ran the opposite tribute logic: while the Aztecs extracted goods, the Inca extracted labor through the mit'a rotation, two empires solving the same problem of tributary extraction with radically different mechanisms.

Motecuzoma II spent the first decade of his reign as the most administratively ambitious tlatoani the empire had produced, centralizing the bureaucracy, standardizing noble titles, expanding the codex tradition, and commissioning the Codex Borbonicus in 1507.

In the second decade, Motecuzoma II withdrew from the council halls, consulted astrologers obsessively, and fixed on the Year One Reed correspondence: Quetzalcoatl had vowed to return in a year called One Reed, and 1519 was that year.

title: The End of the Fifth Sun
bullets: Cortés landed near Veracruz in April 1519 with about 530 men and immediately allied with the Totonacs of Cempoala by arresting their Aztec tribute collectors in public, then scuttled his own ships to eliminate the option of retreat.

Malintzin, a Nahua woman sold into slavery as a child who had learned Yucatec Maya among the Maya of Tabasco, enabled Cortés to communicate with any Nahuatl-speaking polity through a two-step translation chain and became his translator, advisor, and political negotiator.

The Tlaxcalans attacked Cortés's column with 30,000 to 50,000 warriors for two weeks before Xicotencatl the Elder persuaded the senate to ally with the Spanish instead, giving Cortés the most important ally of the conquest and turning the campaign into a civil war within Mesoamerica.

On November 8, 1519, Cortés met Motecuzoma II on the southern causeway, was escorted into the city and housed in the palace of Axayacatl, and within two weeks had taken Motecuzoma hostage in his own palace through simple armed intimidation.

Pedro de Alvarado's Massacre at the Templo Mayor on May 22, 1520 killed several hundred to several thousand unarmed Aztec nobles during the Toxcatl festival, finally turning the entire population of Tenochtitlan openly against the Spanish.

During La Noche Triste on June 30 to July 1, 1520, the Spanish retreat along the Tlacopan causeway cost about 600 European soldiers and thousands of indigenous allies, with many drowning weighed down by stolen gold they refused to abandon.

Smallpox, introduced to the Mexican mainland with the Narváez expedition in April 1520, killed an estimated 25 to 50 percent of the central Mexican population within a year, including Cuitlahuac after only 80 days as tlatoani, wrecking the empire's military and administrative command structure.

Cortés besieged Tenochtitlan from May 26, 1521 with 700 Spanish soldiers and 75,000 to 200,000 indigenous allies, cutting the Chapultepec aqueduct and launching thirteen brigantines on Lake Texcoco, the first European warships on a New World inland lake.

Cuauhtémoc, the last tlatoani, was captured fleeing by canoe on August 13, 1521; tortured by Cortés with boiling oil to reveal the location of the treasury; kept alive as a puppet for years; and executed in 1525, making him modern Mexico's national symbol of indigenous resistance.

The population of central Mexico fell from roughly 12 million in 1519 to about 1 million by 1605, a demographic loss greater than the Black Death in any European country, destroying the civilization beneath the empire even after the political structure was gone.
